title: Governing Fragmentation
subtitle: Jurisdictional Competition and China’s Counter-Extraterritoriality Framework
title_normalized: governing fragmentation: jurisdictional competition and china's counter-extraterritoriality framework

alternate_identifiers.identifier: Governing Fragmentation Jurisdictional Competition and China’s Counter-Extraterritoriality Framework.pdf

alternate_identifiers.description: Short form of the policy brief title

description: This policy brief examines China’s counter-extraterritoriality framework as a structured response to unilateral extraterritorial enforcement and as a signal of wider jurisdictional fragmentation. It argues that jurisdiction is becoming a system-level domain of strategic competition, transforming compliance into a strategic problem for firms, financial institutions, and governance systems.
abstract: China’s Regulation on Countering Unjustified Extraterritorial Jurisdiction marks an escalation in the global contest over legal authority by institutionalizing mechanisms to identify, prohibit, and counter unjustified foreign legal measures. The brief argues that jurisdiction is no longer embedded in a convergent legal order but is emerging as a domain of strategic competition in which states assert, contest, and deny legal authority as an instrument of power. The immediate consequence is fragmentation of global compliance, as firms and financial institutions face increasingly incompatible legal obligations across jurisdictions. These pressures spill over into financial systems, trade networks, platform ecosystems, and cross-border data governance. The brief concludes that fragmentation is not a temporary disruption but a structural feature of an emerging international order defined by competing legal sovereignties and regime coexistence without a central arbiter.

series_information.series_name: EPINOVA Policy Brief Series on Strategic Competition, AI-Enabled Warfare, and Information Conflict
series_information.series_description: Policy brief series translating EPINOVA research on strategic competition, AI-enabled warfare, information conflict, legal fragmentation, and global security governance into concise policy-facing analysis.

methodology.source_basis: Policy-facing conceptual analysis based on China’s counter-extraterritoriality framework and its systemic effects across compliance, financial, trade, technology, and data governance domains.
methodology.case_context: China’s Regulation on Countering Unjustified Extraterritorial Jurisdiction and the broader shift from legal convergence toward competing legal sovereignties.
methodology.core_problem: Global legal and compliance systems increasingly face incompatible jurisdictional commands, making compliance a strategic alignment problem rather than a neutral technical function.
central_claims: Jurisdiction is emerging as a system-level domain of strategic competition.
Global compliance is undergoing structural fragmentation as firms face irreconcilable legal obligations across jurisdictions.
Blocking mechanisms, prohibition orders, and countermeasures function as tools of non-kinetic competition and legal deterrence.
Jurisdictional conflict generates cross-domain spillovers into financial systems, trade networks, technology platforms, and data governance regimes.
The global order is shifting from legal convergence toward regime coexistence among competing, non-hierarchical legal systems.

analytical_lenses.name: Jurisdiction as Strategic Power
analytical_lenses.description: Treats legal authority as a mechanism through which states project influence, constrain external actors, and defend sovereignty.

analytical_lenses.name: Blocking as Legal Strategy
analytical_lenses.description: Interprets prohibition orders and non-compliance mechanisms as active jurisdictional counter-assertion rather than passive non-recognition.

analytical_lenses.name: Compliance Fragmentation
analytical_lenses.description: Analyzes how firms and financial institutions face incompatible obligations and must reorganize compliance as geopolitical risk management.

analytical_lenses.name: Cross-Domain Spillover
analytical_lenses.description: Tracks how legal tensions propagate into financial clearing, supply chains, technology standards, platform ecosystems, and data localization.

analytical_lenses.name: Regime Complex Formation
analytical_lenses.description: Frames the emerging order as a set of overlapping legal regimes without hierarchy, central arbitration, or full convergence.

analytical_lenses.name: Governance of Fragmentation
analytical_lenses.description: Focuses on managing structural tensions and bounded coexistence rather than restoring a unified legal order.

methodology.data_scope: Conceptual and policy analysis based on the PDF’s discussion of legal foundations, compliance conflict, spillover effects, jurisdictional regime complex formation, and medium-term policy trajectories.

dimensions.name: Legal Resistance
dimensions.description: Blocking measures, prohibition orders, and sovereign evaluation of unjustified extraterritorial legal application.

dimensions.name: Compliance Fragmentation
dimensions.description: Divergent legal obligations and corporate exposure to irreconcilable compliance requirements across jurisdictions.

dimensions.name: Cross-Domain Spillover
dimensions.description: Propagation of legal conflict into economic systems, financial clearing, trade structures, technology platforms, and data governance.

dimensions.name: Regime Complex Formation
dimensions.description: Coexistence of competing legal systems in a non-hierarchical jurisdictional order without a central adjudicating authority.

dimensions.name: System Trajectories
dimensions.description: Possible pathways including managed fragmentation, escalating legal retaliation, and systemic bifurcation.

dimensions.name: Governance of Fragmentation
dimensions.description: Institutional and policy efforts to manage structural tensions without assuming legal convergence or systemic unity.

key_findings: China’s counter-extraterritoriality framework relocates assessment of unjustified foreign legal application from an implied international hierarchy to sovereign evaluation.
The framework integrates assessment, prohibition, countermeasures, and judicial remedies into a coordinated enforcement architecture.
Global compliance is moving from rule optimization toward strategic constraint because adherence to one jurisdiction may violate obligations in another.
Firm-level adaptation is a key transmission channel through which legal fragmentation scales into financial, trade, technology, and data governance systems.
The emerging global legal order increasingly resembles a jurisdictional regime complex composed of overlapping, non-hierarchical legal systems.
Counter-extraterritorial frameworks create a paradox: they may stabilize confrontation below kinetic thresholds while reducing systemic predictability and legal coherence.
Medium-term trajectories include managed fragmentation, escalating legal retaliation, and systemic bifurcation, which can coexist and reinforce one another.
The central policy task is not restoring full legal unity, but managing fragmentation so that incompatibility remains bounded rather than systemically destabilizing.
policy_recommendations: Treat jurisdictional competition as a system-level strategic domain rather than a narrow legal compliance issue.
Develop corporate and institutional compliance architectures that can operate under persistent legal incompatibility.
Monitor cross-domain spillovers from legal conflict into financial clearing, supply chains, platform governance, and cross-border data flows.
Design policy responses around managed fragmentation and partial interoperability rather than assumptions of legal convergence.
Strengthen institutional mechanisms for risk differentiation, exemption management, and calibrated coordination to prevent compliance deadlock.
Evaluate counter-extraterritorial instruments not only by immediate legal effect, but by their contribution to regime complex formation and systemic fragmentation.
